text: The development of new precious metal coordination complexes has driven advancements in the fields of lighting, energy production and photocatalysis, among others. Achieving analogous functions using abundant first-row transition metals is therefore highly desirable. To date, most research has focused on the iron(II) ion, here we show that isoelectronic chromium(0) complexes lead to luminescence with higher photoluminescence quantum yields (PLQYs) and excited-state lifetimes than any known first-row d6 system to date. Additionally, their metal-to-ligand charge transfer (MLCT) emission is comparable to benchmarking that of the well-studied and highly active complexes based on the second-row transition metal center, osmium(II) in a polypyridyl system. Their MLCT states can therefore be also efficiently employed for photoredox catalysis. Red light excited photoredox catalysis gives access to benchmark reductions of relevant substrates, confirming that highly efficient low-energy photoactivable catalysts can be obtained using such affordable and abundant transition metal center. Our studies therefore show that complexes with a suitable architecture can be readily and efficiently obtained for the abundant first-row d6 transition metal center and give access to new photophysics and sustainable photoredox chemistry.
